texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 1. Introduction
texts.text: 1. Introduction

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As interest in the environment has increased over the last two decades, restrictions on the thoughtless use of fossil fuels have been tightened accordingly, and the need to develop alternative technologies to replace the use of fossil fuels is being emphasized [1]. Among the various alternative technologies being developed, lithium batteries are drawing special attention as a clean energy resource that can replace fossil fuel. Lithium secondary batteries are used for various mobile devices, such as smart phones, digital cameras and laptop computers [2], and the recent advancement of lithium secondary battery technology enables the replacement of existing electric car batteries with lithium secondary batteries [3]. In addition, lithium micro-batteries are used for medical devices (cardiac pacemakers, hearing aids, and defibrillators) [4]. As lithium batteries are utilized in various industrial sectors, consistent efforts are focused on the further development of these batteries toward completely replacing the use of fossil fuels. As the lithium secondary battery industry is rapidly advancing, the demand for lithium metals is also increasing. The rapid increase of natural lithium production is outstripping the availability of
texts.text: As interest in the environment has increased over the last two decades, restrictions on the thoughtless use of fossil fuels have been tightened accordingly, and the need to develop alternative technologies to replace the use of fossil fuels is being emphasized [1]. Among the various alternative technologies being developed, lithium batteries are drawing special attention as a clean energy resource that can replace fossil fuel. Lithium secondary batteries are used for various mobile devices, such as smart phones, digital cameras and laptop computers [2], and the recent advancement of lithium secondary battery technology enables the replacement of existing electric car batteries with lithium secondary batteries [3]. In addition, lithium micro-batteries are used for medical devices (cardiac pacemakers, hearing aids, and defibrillators) [4]. As lithium batteries are utilized in various industrial sectors, consistent efforts are focused on the further development of these batteries toward completely replacing the use of fossil fuels. As the lithium secondary battery industry is rapidly advancing, the demand for lithium metals is also increasing. The rapid increase of natural lithium production is outstripping the availability of

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: a b s t r a c t
texts.text: a b s t r a c t

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study aims to evaluate the effect of LMO (lithium manganese oxide) and enhanced BEDI (bipolar membrane electro-dialysis deionization) on the lithium ion desorption process for the recovery of lithium ions. The factors that influence lithium ion recovery are evaluated in reference to the pH, voltage, flow rates, and number of bipolar membrane sheets to present the optimal conditions. The research findings show that in the desorption of LMO lithium ions, the lower the pH, the higher the desorption rates. When the voltage was 6.5 V per sheet, the desorption rates were approximately 70%, and when flow rates were 0.44 mL/cm 2 min, the desorption was approximately 30 min faster than that at other flow rates. When the number of bipolar membrane sheets was 4, it was possible to control 4 the pH to under 4, a level at which lithium ions adsorbed onto LMO might affect the desorption phenomenon. For a combination of the conditions above, the desorption efficiency of lithium ions was approximately 70%, and the recovery time was reduced by approximately 180 min compared to when a chemical process was used for lithium ion desorption.
texts.text: This study aims to evaluate the effect of LMO (lithium manganese oxide) and enhanced BEDI (bipolar membrane electro-dialysis deionization) on the lithium ion desorption process for the recovery of lithium ions. The factors that influence lithium ion recovery are evaluated in reference to the pH, voltage, flow rates, and number of bipolar membrane sheets to present the optimal conditions. The research findings show that in the desorption of LMO lithium ions, the lower the pH, the higher the desorption rates. When the voltage was 6.5 V per sheet, the desorption rates were approximately 70%, and when flow rates were 0.44 mL/cm 2 min, the desorption was approximately 30 min faster than that at other flow rates. When the number of bipolar membrane sheets was 4, it was possible to control 4 the pH to under 4, a level at which lithium ions adsorbed onto LMO might affect the desorption phenomenon. For a combination of the conditions above, the desorption efficiency of lithium ions was approximately 70%, and the recovery time was reduced by approximately 180 min compared to when a chemical process was used for lithium ion desorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: the estimated reserves. In particular, 90% of the lithium resource is concentrated in certain regions, such as China, Bolivia, Argentina, and Chile [5], and thus, some even worry that lithium resources might be politically abused.
texts.text: the estimated reserves. In particular, 90% of the lithium resource is concentrated in certain regions, such as China, Bolivia, Argentina, and Chile [5], and thus, some even worry that lithium resources might be politically abused.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As one of the solutions to the drain of the natural lithium ion reserves, the recovery technology of lithium metals from used lithium batteries or from solutions (such as sea water and waste water) that contain lithium metals rather than metallic reserves is attracting increasing attention [6]. The lithium recovery process is divided into the physical process and the chemical process. The physical process is to separate lithium metals from used lithium secondary batteries, and the chemical process is to recover lithium ions from sea water or other solutions. Because the physical process is designed to separate lithium ions only mechanically or physically from compounds of various metallic elements, other metallic ions may be recovered along with lithium ions. In contrast, the chemical process enables selective separation due to the special chemical processing, which is advantageous in recovering high-purity lithium ions [7].
texts.text: As one of the solutions to the drain of the natural lithium ion reserves, the recovery technology of lithium metals from used lithium batteries or from solutions (such as sea water and waste water) that contain lithium metals rather than metallic reserves is attracting increasing attention [6]. The lithium recovery process is divided into the physical process and the chemical process. The physical process is to separate lithium metals from used lithium secondary batteries, and the chemical process is to recover lithium ions from sea water or other solutions. Because the physical process is designed to separate lithium ions only mechanically or physically from compounds of various metallic elements, other metallic ions may be recovered along with lithium ions. In contrast, the chemical process enables selective separation due to the special chemical processing, which is advantageous in recovering high-purity lithium ions [7].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: One research study focused on the inorganic absorbents (LiNi0.5Mn0.5O2, LiMn2O4, and Li1.33Mn1.67O4), which detects lithium ions quite precisely in solutions [8]. For such inorganic absorbents, lithium manganese oxide with a spinel structure contains lithium ions as part of the structure, and thus, ions whose

For such inorganic absorbents, lithium manganese oxide with a spinel structure contains lithium ions as part of the structure, and thus, ions whose

texts.label: text
texts.orig: radius is greater than that of lithium ions cannot enter the structure. Hence, ions other than hydrogen ions, (whose radius is smaller than that of lithium ions), cannot enter the crystal of lithium manganese, thus leading to a high selective adsorption rate to lithium ions [9-11].
texts.text: radius is greater than that of lithium ions cannot enter the structure. Hence, ions other than hydrogen ions, (whose radius is smaller than that of lithium ions), cannot enter the crystal of lithium manganese, thus leading to a high selective adsorption rate to lithium ions [9-11].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When inorganic absorbents are used for lithium ion adsorption under conditions in which the density of lithium ions is low, the adsorption process may take days or even weeks. As a result, the recovery efficiency of lithium ions drastically decreases. This decreasing efficiency is a challenge to be overcome when lithium recovery technology is to be commercialized as inorganic adsorbents. The concept of lithium ion desorption stated above is to replace lithium ions in a manganese oxides with hydrogen ions, which causes lithium ions to leave the absorbent. Because the density of hydrogen ions affects lithium ion desorption, chemicals such as hydrochloric acid are necessary in the process. In addition, an alkali solution is required to neutralize the hydrochloric acid, which raises the problems of recovery efficiency reduction and increased cost [12,13].
texts.text: When inorganic absorbents are used for lithium ion adsorption under conditions in which the density of lithium ions is low, the adsorption process may take days or even weeks. As a result, the recovery efficiency of lithium ions drastically decreases. This decreasing efficiency is a challenge to be overcome when lithium recovery technology is to be commercialized as inorganic adsorbents. The concept of lithium ion desorption stated above is to replace lithium ions in a manganese oxides with hydrogen ions, which causes lithium ions to leave the absorbent. Because the density of hydrogen ions affects lithium ion desorption, chemicals such as hydrochloric acid are necessary in the process. In addition, an alkali solution is required to neutralize the hydrochloric acid, which raises the problems of recovery efficiency reduction and increased cost [12,13].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Hence, this study aims to design an eco-friendly electro-dialysis system that uses no hydrochloric acid as an improvement of the existing LMO absorbent adsorption and desorption process and to apply the system to Neosepta bipolar membranes. In addition, the effect of the system on lithium ion recovery is also evaluated [14].
texts.text: Hence, this study aims to design an eco-friendly electro-dialysis system that uses no hydrochloric acid as an improvement of the existing LMO absorbent adsorption and desorption process and to apply the system to Neosepta bipolar membranes. In addition, the effect of the system on lithium ion recovery is also evaluated [14].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Regarding electro-dialysis devices of bipolar membranes that produce hydrogen and hydroxyl ions by means of a water splitting reaction in the catalytic layer and in the application of voltage, the water splitting characteristic of bipolar membranes is utilized instead of directly using hydrochloric acid solution, as in the case of the existing electro-dialysis systems. Hydrogen ions are provided to extract lithium ions from LMO, and lithium ions are transferred to another section using positive ion exchange membranes. A lithium hydroxide results from the reaction to hydroxyl generated by water splitting.
texts.text: Regarding electro-dialysis devices of bipolar membranes that produce hydrogen and hydroxyl ions by means of a water splitting reaction in the catalytic layer and in the application of voltage, the water splitting characteristic of bipolar membranes is utilized instead of directly using hydrochloric acid solution, as in the case of the existing electro-dialysis systems. Hydrogen ions are provided to extract lithium ions from LMO, and lithium ions are transferred to another section using positive ion exchange membranes. A lithium hydroxide results from the reaction to hydroxyl generated by water splitting.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The variables influencing BEDI (bipolar membrane electrodialysis deionization) are referred to in the voltage setting; in the system designed for this study, the conditions and module size for lithium ion adsorption  desorption are also examined. In addition, the efficiency of lithium ion recovery is evaluated in comparison with the chemical desorption method with the same absorbent used.
texts.text: The variables influencing BEDI (bipolar membrane electrodialysis deionization) are referred to in the voltage setting; in the system designed for this study, the conditions and module size for lithium ion adsorption  desorption are also examined. In addition, the efficiency of lithium ion recovery is evaluated in comparison with the chemical desorption method with the same absorbent used.

texts.orig: 2. Experimental
texts.text: 2. Experimental

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.1. Materials
texts.text: 2.1. Materials

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study involves development of the film used to form the module Neosepta CMX (positive ion exchange membrane, Astom Company, Japan) and Neosepta BP-1 (bipolar membrane, Astom Company, Japan). The properties of the film are presented in Table 1. To produce an electrolyte, Samchun's 98% pure potassium nitrate (KNO3) was used, and to produce a lithium salts solution, Aldrich's 99% pure lithium chloride (LiCl) was used. Aldrich's 96% pure LiMn2O4 (lithium manganese oxide) was used as the inorganic adsorbent. None of the reagents used in the experiment underwent a separate refining process.
texts.text: This study involves development of the film used to form the module Neosepta CMX (positive ion exchange membrane, Astom Company, Japan) and Neosepta BP-1 (bipolar membrane, Astom Company, Japan). The properties of the film are presented in Table 1. To produce an electrolyte, Samchun's 98% pure potassium nitrate (KNO3) was used, and to produce a lithium salts solution, Aldrich's 99% pure lithium chloride (LiCl) was used. Aldrich's 96% pure LiMn2O4 (lithium manganese oxide) was used as the inorganic adsorbent. None of the reagents used in the experiment underwent a separate refining process.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.2. Module design
texts.text: 2.2. Module design

texts.label: text
texts.orig: As shown in Fig. 1(c), the BEDI module consisted of platinumcoated titanium electrodes, cation exchange membranes, and 3mm-thick spacers made of PVC (polyvinyl chloride) for flow path setting. To protect the ion exchange membrane between films and between a film and a spacer, used was a 1-mm-thick separator made of PP (polypropylene) was used. With a bipolar membrane (BP) and a cation exchange membrane (C) alternating between the anode and cathode, a total of 8 compartment cells were formed. In addition, 2 electrolytic cells, 3 desorption parts, and 3 concentration parts were formed. The film was 12 cm -13 cm in size. The volumes of concentration part and desorption part are equally 500 mL.
texts.text: As shown in Fig. 1(c), the BEDI module consisted of platinumcoated titanium electrodes, cation exchange membranes, and 3mm-thick spacers made of PVC (polyvinyl chloride) for flow path setting. To protect the ion exchange membrane between films and between a film and a spacer, used was a 1-mm-thick separator made of PP (polypropylene) was used. With a bipolar membrane (BP) and a cation exchange membrane (C) alternating between the anode and cathode, a total of 8 compartment cells were formed. In addition, 2 electrolytic cells, 3 desorption parts, and 3 concentration parts were formed. The film was 12 cm -13 cm in size. The volumes of concentration part and desorption part are equally 500 mL.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: At both electrodes, voltage was provided by means of a power supply, with a voltage range between 0 and 30 V. Each module was divided into sections by films, and the sections played three different roles: desorption, concentration, and electrolyte. On the plane where bipolar membrane hydrogen ions are discharged and in the area between the two ion exchange membranes, LMO was inserted and allowed to move randomly. During this period, LMO reacted with the hydrogen ions, which resulted in the desorption of lithium ions from the LMO and their transfer to the concentration part through the cation exchange membrane by the difference of electric potential. Potassium nitrate solution, which was used as an electrolyte, ran in front of the two pole plates. The sections were separated by bipolar membranes, so that the ions in the electrolyte could not flow into the desorption part or the concentration part. The electrolytic cell, the desorption part, and the concentration part were connected to a 1-L acryl water tank, and the solution from the module was recycled back to the acryl tank for continuous desorption in the circulation system.
texts.text: At both electrodes, voltage was provided by means of a power supply, with a voltage range between 0 and 30 V. Each module was divided into sections by films, and the sections played three different roles: desorption, concentration, and electrolyte. On the plane where bipolar membrane hydrogen ions are discharged and in the area between the two ion exchange membranes, LMO was inserted and allowed to move randomly. During this period, LMO reacted with the hydrogen ions, which resulted in the desorption of lithium ions from the LMO and their transfer to the concentration part through the cation exchange membrane by the difference of electric potential. Potassium nitrate solution, which was used as an electrolyte, ran in front of the two pole plates. The sections were separated by bipolar membranes, so that the ions in the electrolyte could not flow into the desorption part or the concentration part. The electrolytic cell, the desorption part, and the concentration part were connected to a 1-L acryl water tank, and the solution from the module was recycled back to the acryl tank for continuous desorption in the circulation system.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A magnetic pump was used to prevent the LMO from lumping together and circulating in the system. The module was designed to increase the probability of reaction to hydrogen ions. The module's flow path was designed diagonally so that the detention time of solution in the module could increase [15,16].
texts.text: A magnetic pump was used to prevent the LMO from lumping together and circulating in the system. The module was designed to increase the probability of reaction to hydrogen ions. The module's flow path was designed diagonally so that the detention time of solution in the module could increase [15,16].

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.3. Experiment of lithium ion chemical desorption
texts.text: 2.3. Experiment of lithium ion chemical desorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: A chemical desorption experiment was conducted to compare the results with those of the BEDI module-based desorption experiment. To extract lithium ions using the LMO, which is an inorganic adsorbent for lithium adsorption, 23-h mixing was implemented in 500 mL of 1 M HCl solution. To separate the LMO from the HCl solution, filtering was followed by cleaning with distilled water and then with methanol. The product was dried at 80 ° C for 4 h in an oven.
texts.text: A chemical desorption experiment was conducted to compare the results with those of the BEDI module-based desorption experiment. To extract lithium ions using the LMO, which is an inorganic adsorbent for lithium adsorption, 23-h mixing was implemented in 500 mL of 1 M HCl solution. To separate the LMO from the HCl solution, filtering was followed by cleaning with distilled water and then with methanol. The product was dried at 80 ° C for 4 h in an oven.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To produce the lithium ion solution, 2.5 L of distilled water was used to dissolve 1.25 g of LiCl to produce LiCl solution. The LiCl solution was divided into 5 equal parts to make 5 samples, each of which had a different pH value ranging from 2 to 6, with a pH step of 1 by adding small amounts of 0.1 M HCl solution. Under the condition above, lithium ion desorption was implemented for 8 h, and sampling was conducted at intervals of 2 h. The lithium ion densities of collected samples were measured by ICP (inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer). The desorption rates of lithium ions were calculated using the following expression [17]:

The lithium ion densities of collected samples were measured by ICP (inductively coupled plasma atomic emission spectrometer). texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 2.4. BEDI module water decomposition experiment
texts.text: 2.4. BEDI module water decomposition experiment

texts.label: text
texts.orig: To evaluate the effect of pH on lithium ion desorption, the number of bipolar membranes and cation exchange membranes necessary for the module was increased from 2 to 4. With the inflow rates constant, the flow rates per unit area were changed. The three modules and the diagram of bipolar membranes are presented in Fig. 1. Fig. 1(a) shows the module that consisted of 2 bipolar membranes and 1 cation exchange membrane. With the voltage adjusted between 4 and 7 V per sheet at intervals of 1 V, the water splitting of bipolar membranes was observed. Fig. 1(b) shows the module that consisted of 3 bipolar membranes and 2 cation exchange membranes. The range of voltage was the same as that of the module with 2 bipolar membranes. Fig. 1(c) shows the module that consisted of 4 bipolar membranes and 3 cation exchange membranes. The range of voltage used was the same for each case. Left sides of cation membranes are desorption parts and Right sides of anion membranes are concentration parts. A 0.5 M KNO3 solution was used as the electrolytes for all modules, and the changes in desorption part pH were measured using a pH meter (see Fig. 2).
texts.text: To evaluate the effect of pH on lithium ion desorption, the number of bipolar membranes and cation exchange membranes necessary for the module was increased from 2 to 4. Fig. 1(b) shows the module that consisted of 3 bipolar membranes and 2 cation exchange membranes. The range of voltage was the same as that of the module with 2 bipolar membranes. Fig. 1(c) shows the module that consisted of 4 bipolar membranes and 3 cation exchange membranes. The range of voltage used was the same for each case. Left sides of cation membranes are desorption parts and Right sides of anion membranes are concentration parts. A 0.5 M KNO3 solution was used as the electrolytes for all modules, and the changes in desorption part pH were measured using a pH meter (see Fig. 2).

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.1. Experiment of lithium ion chemical desorption
texts.text: 3.1. Experiment of lithium ion chemical desorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 3 shows the results of the chemical desorption experiment conducted to examine the correlation between LMO lithium ion desorption and the density of hydrogen ions. As shown in Fig. 3, under the conditions involving pH values of 2 and 3 and for 8-h of desorption, the desorption rates were over 60% . Especially In particular, in under the condition of pH 2, the desorption rates were over 85 %. However, under the conditions of pH values of 4, 5, and 6 and for 8-h of desorption, the desorption rates were low, down to 10%. These results corresponds to those of Lemaire, which demonstrated that the higher the density of hydrogen ions, the better the lithium ion desorption [20].
texts.text: Fig. 3 shows the results of the chemical desorption experiment conducted to examine the correlation between LMO lithium ion desorption and the density of hydrogen ions. As shown in Fig. 3, under the conditions involving pH values of 2 and 3 and for 8-h of desorption, the desorption rates were over 60% . Especially In particular, in under the condition of pH 2, the desorption rates were over 85 %. However, under the conditions of pH values of 4, 5, and 6 and for 8-h of desorption, the desorption rates were low, down to 10%. These results corresponds to those of Lemaire, which demonstrated that the higher the density of hydrogen ions, the better the lithium ion desorption [20].

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This type of desorption reaction may be expressed in the following chemical reaction formula proposed by Wang et al. [21,22]
texts.text: This type of desorption reaction may be expressed in the following chemical reaction formula proposed by Wang et al. [21,22]

texts.label: formula
texts.orig: LiMn2O4 þ H þ ! Li ð 1 � x Þ H x Mn2O4 þ H þ ð 1 � x Þ þ Li x

texts.label: text
texts.orig: When the hydrogen ion density of solution increases after Li ions are adsorbed onto a manganese oxide type of spinel structure, the substitution with lithium ions results in lithium ion desorption. The reaction formula corresponds to the reaction curves of chemically processed lithium desorption shown in Fig. 3. Such desorption curves are typical lithium ion desorption curves, whose desorption rates increase as the density of hydrogen ions increase, as shown in Fig. 3.
texts.text: When the hydrogen ion density of solution increases after Li ions are adsorbed onto a manganese oxide type of spinel structure, the substitution with lithium ions results in lithium ion desorption. The reaction formula corresponds to the reaction curves of chemically processed lithium desorption shown in Fig. 3. Such desorption curves are typical lithium ion desorption curves, whose desorption rates increase as the density of hydrogen ions increase, as shown in Fig. 3.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.2. Water splitting experiment in a BEDI module
texts.text: 3.2. Water splitting experiment in a BEDI module

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 4 shows changes in pH, which is one of the desorption factors of influence of lithium ions, depending on the voltage at different flow rates per area of the BEDI module. As shown in this figure, regarding the changes in module pH, the lower flow rates per unit area, resulted in the higher density of hydrogen ions. This effect probably occurred because, as the detention time of a module in the bipolar membrane area increased, the time of contact with hydrogen ions in the bipolar membrane at the same flux increased accordingly. To adjust the pH for the desorption experiment of lithium ions in LMO, it seems that a voltage over 6 V per sheet must be applied in view of the experiment of chemical desorption of lithium ions. The experimental result above shows that, because the flow rates per unit area were low, the inflection point in the pH curves occurred at a lower voltage.
texts.text: Fig. 4 shows changes in pH, which is one of the desorption factors of influence of lithium ions, depending on the voltage at different flow rates per area of the BEDI module. As shown in this figure, regarding the changes in module pH, the lower flow rates per unit area, resulted in the higher density of hydrogen ions. This effect probably occurred because, as the detention time of a module in the bipolar membrane area increased, the time of contact with hydrogen ions in the bipolar membrane at the same flux increased accordingly. To adjust the pH for the desorption experiment of lithium ions in LMO, it seems that a voltage over 6 V per sheet must be applied in view of the experiment of chemical desorption of lithium ions. The experimental result above shows that, because the flow rates per unit area were low, the inflection point in the pH curves occurred at a lower voltage.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: It was thought that the pH in the condition of 7 V per sheet would be lower than that in the condition of 6 V, but these values were found to be the same, probably because the diffusion as a result of the increasing density of hydrogen ions caused hydrogen ions to move to the concentration part through the cation exchange membrane. Hence, it is expected that the pH in the actual desorption process would be higher than the measured value.
texts.text: It was thought that the pH in the condition of 7 V per sheet would be lower than that in the condition of 6 V, but these values were found to be the same, probably because the diffusion as a result of the increasing density of hydrogen ions caused hydrogen ions to move to the concentration part through the cation exchange membrane. Hence, it is expected that the pH in the actual desorption process would be higher than the measured value.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 5 shows changes in pH, which are a factor of influence of the desorption of lithium ions, with different voltage applied to the BEDI-4 module that was formed by means of the bipolar membranes mentioned in Section 4. This figure shows that when 6 V or higher voltage per sheet was applied, the phenomenon of water decomposition took place and decreased the pH as a result. In the condition of 6 V or 7 V per sheet, the pH decreased by 3.5 after 30 min. This result corresponds to the findings of Tanaka's research, in which a phenomenon of water decomposition of bipolar membranes would increase the constant of water decomposition as the voltage increased [23]. In addition, the results of the experiment of lithium ion chemical desorption indicates that a higher density of hydrogen ions would improve the desorption of lithium ions from the LMO. Thus, it seems efficient to apply 6 V for approximately 30 min in the lithium desorption experiment because the density of hydrogen ions was the highest at this level.
texts.text: Fig. 5 shows changes in pH, which are a factor of influence of the desorption of lithium ions, with different voltage applied to the BEDI-4 module that was formed by means of the bipolar membranes mentioned in Section 4. This figure shows that when 6 V or higher voltage per sheet was applied, the phenomenon of water decomposition took place and decreased the pH as a result. In the condition of 6 V or 7 V per sheet, the pH decreased by 3.5 after 30 min. This result corresponds to the findings of Tanaka's research, in which a phenomenon of water decomposition of bipolar membranes would increase the constant of water decomposition as the voltage increased [23]. In addition, the results of the experiment of lithium ion chemical desorption indicates that a higher density of hydrogen ions would improve the desorption of lithium ions from the LMO. Thus, it seems efficient to apply 6 V for approximately 30 min in the lithium desorption experiment because the density of hydrogen ions was the highest at this level.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.3. Experiment of lithium ion electric desorption
texts.text: 3.3. Experiment of lithium ion electric desorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 6 shows the desorption rates of lithium ions obtained with voltage as a variable by means of BEDI-4, which were proven by the water decomposition experiment as being appropriate for an electric desorption experiment. The higher the voltage, the higher the desorption rates of lithium ions. In particular, there was little difference in the desorption rates before the section of 30 min, but from at 6 V per sheet, a difference in desorption rates was observed. It is thought that under the condition of 6 V per sheet or higher, the water decomposition of bipolar membranes increased the density of hydrogen ions compared with the other voltage conditions, which increased the probability of reactions between LMO and hydrogen ions and thus increased the desorption rates. In view of these results, lithium desorption of LMO was found to be closely associated with the pH thus, for LMO desorption, the lower pH of the desorption part, the more efficient the reaction. For a desorption of lithium ions, that reaches as much as 50% within 120 min, at least 6 V per sheet must be applied. The higher voltage, the greater the amount of lithium desorption.
texts.text: Fig. 6 shows the desorption rates of lithium ions obtained with voltage as a variable by means of BEDI-4, which were proven by the water decomposition experiment as being appropriate for an electric desorption experiment. The higher the voltage, the higher the desorption rates of lithium ions. In particular, there was little difference in the desorption rates before the section of 30 min, but from at 6 V per sheet, a difference in desorption rates was observed. It is thought that under the condition of 6 V per sheet or higher, the water decomposition of bipolar membranes increased the density of hydrogen ions compared with the other voltage conditions, which increased the probability of reactions between LMO and hydrogen ions and thus increased the desorption rates. In view of these results, lithium desorption of LMO was found to be closely associated with the pH thus, for LMO desorption, the lower pH of the desorption part, the more efficient the reaction. For a desorption of lithium ions, that reaches as much as 50% within 120 min, at least 6 V per sheet must be applied. The higher voltage, the greater the amount of lithium desorption.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: The result of the lithium electric desorption experiment shows that the highest desorption rates were observed in the condition of 7 V per sheet, whereas the current efficiency calculation result shows that the desorption rates were best in the condition of 6.5 V per sheet, probably because the density of lithium ion desorption did not increased in proportion to the changing current. Hence, to maintain the current efficiency over 60%, at least 6 V per sheet must be applied to the module, and 6.5 V per sheet would be the most effective.
texts.text: The result of the lithium electric desorption experiment shows that the highest desorption rates were observed in the condition of 7 V per sheet, whereas the current efficiency calculation result shows that the desorption rates were best in the condition of 6.5 V per sheet, probably because the density of lithium ion desorption did not increased in proportion to the changing current. Hence, to maintain the current efficiency over 60%, at least 6 V per sheet must be applied to the module, and 6.5 V per sheet would be the most effective.

texts.label: text
texts.orig: rates as a variable, and the desorption rates were measured. The results indicated that the lower the flow rates per unit area, the faster the desorption. In particular, at 60 min, the desorption rates at the highest flow rates were approximately 20% different from those at the lowest flow rates, probably because desorption occurred faster as the time of contact with the ion exchange membrane in the module and the detention time in the module increased. After 120 min, however, the desorption rates were almost the same, probably because desorption would no longer occur after a certain time of detention. To maintain high desorption rates, therefore, the relative flow rates must be kept low in a range of speed where the absorbent could move within that module.
texts.text: rates as a variable, and the desorption rates were measured. The results indicated that the lower the flow rates per unit area, the faster the desorption. In particular, at 60 min, the desorption rates at the highest flow rates were approximately 20% different from those at the lowest flow rates, probably because desorption occurred faster as the time of contact with the ion exchange membrane in the module and the detention time in the module increased. After 120 min, however, the desorption rates were almost the same, probably because desorption would no longer occur after a certain time of detention. To maintain high desorption rates, therefore, the relative flow rates must be kept low in a range of speed where the absorbent could move within that module.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 3.6. Comparison of lithium ion electric desorption and chemical desorption
texts.text: 3.6. Comparison of lithium ion electric desorption and chemical desorption

texts.label: text
texts.orig: Fig. 9 shows the results of comparing the desorption rates of lithium ions over time with the same LMO applied. The electric desorption graph shows desorption rates under the condition of 7 V per sheet, while the chemical desorption graph show desorption rates at pH 2. In the case of chemical desorption, the desorption rates continued to increase, while the electric desorption rates stopped increasing after 120 min. In the case of electric desorption, approximately 180 min were saved compared with chemical desorption, until it reached approximately 70% of desorption rates. After 120 min of operation of the BEDI module, which is an electric desorption system, the desorption rates were approximately 30% higher than those of chemical desorption. Thus, it is expected that applying the circulation system at intervals of 120 min would be efficient.
texts.text: Fig. 9 shows the results of comparing the desorption rates of lithium ions over time with the same LMO applied. The electric desorption graph shows desorption rates under the condition of 7 V per sheet, while the chemical desorption graph show desorption rates at pH 2. In the case of chemical desorption, the desorption rates continued to increase, while the electric desorption rates stopped increasing after 120 min. In the case of electric desorption, approximately 180 min were saved compared with chemical desorption, until it reached approximately 70% of desorption rates. After 120 min of operation of the BEDI module, which is an electric desorption system, the desorption rates were approximately 30% higher than those of chemical desorption. Thus, it is expected that applying the circulation system at intervals of 120 min would be efficient.

texts.label: section_header
texts.orig: 4. Conclusions
texts.text: 4. Conclusions

texts.label: text
texts.orig: This study aimed to design a lithium ion desorption module based on bipolar membranes for a recovery process. In addition, chemical desorption and electric desorption of lithium ions were compared using this module, and the conclusions are as follows:
texts.text: This study aimed to design a lithium ion desorption module based on bipolar membranes for a recovery process. In addition, chemical desorption and electric desorption of lithium ions were compared using this module, and the conclusions are as follows:

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 1. Under the condition of pH 3 or lower, LMO lithium ions affected desorption, and the highest desorption rates were approximately 85%.
texts.text: Under the condition of pH 3 or lower, LMO lithium ions affected desorption, and the highest desorption rates were approximately 85%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 2. In the experiment of water decomposition versus voltage, the range of decomposition voltage of the used bipolar membranes was 5-6 V. For desorption of at least 50% of lithium ions from LMO, the module had to maintain a pH of less than 4. To this end, the flow rates per unit area in the module must be set to 0.44 mL/cm 2 min with at least 6 V per sheet applied.
texts.text: In the experiment of water decomposition versus voltage, the range of decomposition voltage of the used bipolar membranes was 5-6 V. For desorption of at least 50% of lithium ions from LMO, the module had to maintain a pH of less than 4. To this end, the flow rates per unit area in the module must be set to 0.44 mL/cm 2 min with at least 6 V per sheet applied.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 3. The result of the electric desorption experiment shows that at least 6.5 V per sheet must be applied to ensure the desorption of at least 60% of lithium ions from LMO. The optimal current efficiency was found to be 6.5 V per sheet.
texts.text: The result of the electric desorption experiment shows that at least 6.5 V per sheet must be applied to ensure the desorption of at least 60% of lithium ions from LMO. The optimal current efficiency was found to be 6.5 V per sheet.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 4. In the experiment of water decomposition versus flow rates, approximately 30 min were saved in the condition of 0.44 mL/ cm 2 min, i.e., the lowest flow rates able to secure the liquidity of the absorbent, compared to the condition of 1.28 mL/cm 2 -min, i.e., the highest flow rate. Regarding the effect of the lithium ion desorption efficiency, the flow rates of 0.44 mL/ cm 2 min resulted in the highest efficiency, up to 70%.
texts.text: In the experiment of water decomposition versus flow rates, approximately 30 min were saved in the condition of 0.44 mL/ cm 2 min, i.e., the lowest flow rates able to secure the liquidity of the absorbent, compared to the condition of 1.28 mL/cm 2 -min, i.e., the highest flow rate. Regarding the effect of the lithium ion desorption efficiency, the flow rates of 0.44 mL/ cm 2 min resulted in the highest efficiency, up to 70%.

texts.label: list_item
texts.orig: 5. The BEDI module designed in this study for lithium ion recovery was demonstrated to save approximately 180 min compared to chemical desorption when the operation conditions of the module were as follows: pH less than 4; the number of sheets of bipolar membranes: 4; voltage per sheet: 6.5 V; and flow rate: 0.44 mL/cm 2 min.
texts.text: The BEDI module designed in this study for lithium ion recovery was demonstrated to save approximately 180 min compared to chemical desorption when the operation conditions of the module were as follows: pH less than 4; the number of sheets of bipolar membranes: 4; voltage per sheet: 6.5 V; and flow rate: 0.44 mL/cm 2 min.
